subject: Mathematics
title: Square of Sum
explanation: The square of the sum of two terms. Expand by multiplying (a+b) by itself. The middle term is twice the product of the two terms.

subject: Mathematics
title: Square of Difference
explanation: The square of the difference of two terms. The middle term is negative. Note: (a-b)² is always non-negative.

subject: Mathematics
title: Difference of Squares
explanation: Any difference of two perfect squares factors into the product of their sum and difference. Extremely useful for quick factorisation and simplification.

subject: Mathematics
title: Square of a Trinomial
explanation: Expansion of the square of a three-term expression. Contains the sum of squares of all terms plus twice the product of every distinct pair.

subject: Mathematics
title: Cube of Sum
explanation: Cube of sum of two terms. Can also be written as a³ + b³ + 3ab(a + b). Useful in simplifying cubic expressions.

subject: Mathematics
title: Cube of Difference
explanation: Cube of difference of two terms. Can also be written as a³ - b³ - 3ab(a - b). Signs alternate starting with positive.

subject: Mathematics
title: Sum of Cubes
explanation: Factorisation of sum of two cubes. The second factor a² - ab + b² cannot be factorised further over real numbers.

subject: Mathematics
title: Difference of Cubes
explanation: Factorisation of difference of two cubes. The second factor a² + ab + b² has no real roots and cannot be factorised further.

subject: Mathematics
title: Conditional Cube Identity
latexCode: \text{If } a + b + c = 0, \text{ then } a^3 + b^3 + c^3 = 3abc
explanation: A direct consequence of the three-variable cube expansion. Used as a powerful shortcut when the sum of three quantities is zero.

subject: Mathematics
title: Sum of Three Cubes (General)
explanation: General identity for three cubes. When a + b + c = 0, the right side becomes zero, reducing to the conditional identity a³+b³+c³ = 3abc.

subject: Mathematics
title: Sum of Squares — Derived Form
explanation: Express sum of squares in terms of sum/difference and product. Used when a+b and ab are known but a and b individually are not.

subject: Mathematics
title: Product via Sum and Difference
explanation: The difference between square of sum and square of difference always equals four times the product ab. Useful for finding ab when (a+b) and (a-b) are known.

subject: Mathematics
title: Reciprocal Identity — Square
explanation: Identities involving a variable and its reciprocal. If x + 1/x = k, then x² + 1/x² = k² − 2. Very frequently asked in Class 9 and 10.

subject: Mathematics
title: Reciprocal Identity — Cube
explanation: Cube form of reciprocal identities. If x + 1/x = k, then x³ + 1/x³ = k³ − 3k. Frequently asked in Class 9–10 exams.

subject: Mathematics
title: Perfect Square Trinomial Recognition
explanation: A trinomial is a perfect square if the middle term equals twice the product of the square roots of the first and last terms.

subject: Mathematics
title: Difference of Fourth Powers
explanation: Obtained by applying the difference of squares identity twice: a⁴ - b⁴ = (a²+b²)(a²-b²) = (a²+b²)(a+b)(a-b).

subject: Mathematics
title: Sophie Germain Identity
explanation: A classic factorisation of a⁴ + 4b⁴, which appears to be a sum but can still be factored. Frequently appears in olympiad and JEE Advanced problems.

subject: Mathematics
title: Difference of nth Powers
explanation: (a - b) is always a factor of aⁿ - bⁿ for any positive integer n. When n is even, (a + b) is also a factor. Used in number theory and series.

subject: Mathematics
title: Sum of nth Powers (Odd n only)
latexCode: a^n + b^n = (a + b)(a^{n-1} - a^{n-2}b + \cdots + b^{n-1}) \quad (n \text{ odd})
explanation: (a + b) is a factor of aⁿ + bⁿ only when n is odd. The signs in the second factor alternate. For even n, aⁿ + bⁿ cannot be factored over reals.

subject: Mathematics
title: AM-GM Inequality
explanation: Arithmetic Mean is always ≥ Geometric Mean for non-negative numbers. Equality holds only when a = b. Used in finding minimum values of expressions.

subject: Mathematics
title: Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality
explanation: The product of sums of squares is always ≥ the square of the sum of cross products. Used in Class 11–12 and JEE to find minimum/maximum values.

subject: Mathematics
title: Completing the Square
explanation: Rewriting a quadratic in vertex form. Used to find the minimum/maximum value of a quadratic expression and to derive the quadratic formula.

subject: Mathematics
title: Discriminant & Quadratic Formula
explanation: D > 0: two distinct real roots. D = 0: two equal real roots. D < 0: no real roots (complex). One of the most important results in all of algebra.

subject: Mathematics
title: Vieta's Formulas — Quadratic
explanation: Relates roots to coefficients without solving the equation. Used to find α²+β², α³+β³, αβ²+α²β and similar symmetric expressions instantly.

subject: Mathematics
title: Vieta's Formulas — Cubic
explanation: Relations between roots and coefficients for a cubic. Used to find symmetric expressions of roots in Class 11–12 problems.

subject: Mathematics
title: Power Sum of Roots — p₂ and p₃
explanation: Express power sums of roots using sum (S₁ = α+β) and product (S₂ = αβ). Avoids solving for individual roots. Very common in board exams.

subject: Mathematics
title: Binomial Theorem — General Expansion
explanation: General formula to expand any positive integer power of a binomial. The binomial coefficient C(n,k) = n! / (k!(n-k)!). Core topic of Class 11 Mathematics.

subject: Mathematics
title: Binomial Theorem — General Term
explanation: The (r+1)th term in the expansion of (x+y)ⁿ. Used to find any specific term without expanding the full binomial. Essential for Class 11 problems.

subject: Mathematics
title: Binomial Coefficient Sum Identities
explanation: Sum of all binomial coefficients = 2ⁿ (substitute x=y=1). Alternating sum = 0 (substitute x=1, y=−1). Both are standard Class 11 results.

subject: Mathematics
title: Middle Term in Binomial Expansion
latexCode: \begin{aligned} &n \text{ even} \Rightarrow \text{middle term} = T_{\frac{n}{2}+1} \\ &n \text{ odd} \Rightarrow \text{two middle terms: } T_{\frac{n+1}{2}} \text{ and } T_{\frac{n+3}{2}} \end{aligned}
explanation: One middle term when n is even, two middle terms when n is odd. A standard rule asked directly in Class 11 board and competitive exams.

subject: Mathematics
title: Pascal's Triangle Recurrence
explanation: Each entry in Pascal's triangle equals the sum of the two entries directly above it. The foundation of binomial coefficient calculations in Class 11.

subject: Mathematics
title: Symmetric Function — Sum of Squares (3 vars)
explanation: Rearrangement of the trinomial square. Used in Class 9–11 to find a²+b²+c² when the sum and pairwise products are known.

subject: Mathematics
title: Lagrange Identity
explanation: Extends the Cauchy–Schwarz identity. The extra term (a₁b₂ - a₂b₁)² is zero only when the two pairs are proportional. Used in advanced algebra and vectors.

subject: Mathematics
title: Cube of a Trinomial — Compact Form
explanation: Elegant compact form of the cube of a trinomial. Directly gives a³+b³+c³ = (a+b+c)³ − 3(a+b)(b+c)(c+a). Useful in olympiad problems.

subject: Mathematics
title: Factorisation — Sum of Three Squares Condition
latexCode: a^2 + b^2 + c^2 - ab - bc - ca = \frac{1}{2}\left[(a-b)^2 + (b-c)^2 + (c-a)^2\right]
explanation: This expression is always non-negative since it is half the sum of squares of differences. It equals zero only when a = b = c. Used in inequality proofs.

subject: Mathematics
title: Basic Definitions — Right Triangle
explanation: The three primary trigonometric ratios defined for an acute angle θ in a right triangle. Remember using SOH-CAH-TOA: Sin = Opposite/Hypotenuse, Cos = Adjacent/Hypotenuse, Tan = Opposite/Adjacent.

subject: Mathematics
title: Basic Definitions — Remaining Three Functions
explanation: The three secondary trigonometric ratios. Cot, Sec, and Cosec are reciprocals of Tan, Cos, and Sin respectively. All six ratios are defined from the same right triangle.

subject: Mathematics
title: Standard Angle Values
explanation: Standard values for sin, cos, tan at 0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90°. Trick: sin values are √0/2, √1/2, √2/2, √3/2, √4/2 = 0, 1/2, 1/√2, √3/2, 1. Cos values are exactly reverse.

subject: Mathematics
title: Signs in Four Quadrants (ASTC Rule)
latexCode: \begin{aligned} &\text{Quadrant I (0° to 90°): All positive} \\ &\text{Quadrant II (90° to 180°): Sin positive} \\ &\text{Quadrant III (180° to 270°): Tan positive} \\ &\text{Quadrant IV (270° to 360°): Cos positive} \end{aligned}
explanation: ASTC rule: All Students Take Calculus — All, Sin, Tan, Cos are positive in Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4 respectively. Their reciprocals have the same sign as the parent function in each quadrant.

subject: Mathematics
title: Reciprocal Identities — All Six
explanation: Each trigonometric function is the reciprocal of exactly one other. Note: sin and cos are NOT reciprocals of each other. These identities hold for all values where the functions are defined.

subject: Mathematics
title: Pythagorean Identity — sin and cos
explanation: The fundamental Pythagorean identity. Follows directly from the Pythagorean theorem applied to a unit circle: if a point on the unit circle has coordinates (cos θ, sin θ), then x² + y² = 1.

subject: Mathematics
title: Pythagorean Identity — tan and sec
explanation: Obtained by dividing sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 by cos²θ. Valid for all θ where cos θ ≠ 0. The factored form sec²θ − tan²θ = (sec θ + tan θ)(sec θ − tan θ) = 1 is useful in integration.

subject: Mathematics
title: Pythagorean Identity — cot and cosec
explanation: Obtained by dividing sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 by sin²θ. Valid for all θ where sin θ ≠ 0. All three Pythagorean identities can be rearranged to express any one function in terms of another.

subject: Mathematics
title: Quotient Identities
explanation: Tan and cot are expressed as ratios of sin and cos. These are derived from the right-triangle definitions. They are used constantly to simplify trigonometric expressions and prove identities.

subject: Mathematics
title: Complementary Angle Identities (90° − θ)
explanation: Co-function identities: the trig function of an angle equals the co-function of its complement (90° − θ). Sin↔Cos, Tan↔Cot, Sec↔Cosec are the complementary pairs.

subject: Mathematics
title: Supplementary Angle Identities (180° − θ)
explanation: For (180° − θ): only sin and cosec remain positive (second quadrant). All others become negative. The function name does NOT change (sin stays sin, cos stays cos, etc.).

subject: Mathematics
title: Identities for (180° + θ) and (270° − θ)
explanation: For (180°+θ): only tan and cot positive (third quadrant). Function name unchanged. For (270°−θ): third quadrant, function name changes (sin↔cos, tan↔cot).

subject: Mathematics
title: Identities for (270° + θ) and (360° − θ)
explanation: For (270°+θ): fourth quadrant, function name changes. For (360°−θ): fourth quadrant, function name unchanged (only cos and sec positive). 360°−θ behaves like −θ.

subject: Mathematics
title: Negative Angle Identities — Odd and Even Functions
explanation: Cos and sec are even functions (symmetric about y-axis). Sin, tan, cot, cosec are odd functions (point symmetry about origin). Even: f(−x) = f(x). Odd: f(−x) = −f(x).

subject: Mathematics
title: Sum and Difference — sin
latexCode: \begin{aligned} \sin(A+B) &= \sin A\cos B + \cos A\sin B \\ \sin(A-B) &= \sin A\cos B - \cos A\sin B \end{aligned}
explanation: Sine of sum/difference formulas. Both involve products of sines and cosines with mixed angles. The sign in the formula matches the sign of the angle operation (+ stays +, − changes to −).

subject: Mathematics
title: Sum and Difference — cos
latexCode: \begin{aligned} \cos(A+B) &= \cos A\cos B - \sin A\sin B \\ \cos(A-B) &= \cos A\cos B + \sin A\sin B \end{aligned}
explanation: Cosine of sum/difference formulas. Unlike sin formulas, here the sign is OPPOSITE to the angle operation: cos(A+B) has a minus sign and cos(A−B) has a plus sign.

subject: Mathematics
title: Sum and Difference — tan
latexCode: \begin{aligned} \tan(A+B) &= \frac{\tan A + \tan B}{1 - \tan A\tan B} \\ \tan(A-B) &= \frac{\tan A - \tan B}{1 + \tan A\tan B} \end{aligned}
explanation: Tangent addition and subtraction formulas. The denominator uses the opposite sign: (A+B) has 1 minus product in denominator, (A−B) has 1 plus product. Undefined when denominator = 0.

subject: Mathematics
title: Sum and Difference — cot
latexCode: \begin{aligned} \cot(A+B) &= \frac{\cot A\cot B - 1}{\cot A + \cot B} \\ \cot(A-B) &= \frac{\cot A\cot B + 1}{\cot B - \cot A} \end{aligned}
explanation: Cotangent addition and subtraction formulas. The numerator uses the opposite sign convention to tan formulas. Less commonly tested but useful for problems involving cot directly.

subject: Mathematics
title: Double Angle — sin
explanation: Derived from sin(A+A). The second form in terms of tan A is obtained using sin A = tan A/sec A = tan A/√(1+tan²A) and cos A = 1/√(1+tan²A). Both forms are used in integration and equation solving.

subject: Mathematics
title: Double Angle — cos (Three Forms)
latexCode: \begin{aligned} \cos 2A &= \cos^2 A - \sin^2 A \\ &= 2\cos^2 A - 1 \\ &= 1 - 2\sin^2 A \\ &= \frac{1 - \tan^2 A}{1 + \tan^2 A} \end{aligned}
explanation: cos 2A has four equivalent forms — all important. The 2nd and 3rd forms are used to derive the power-reduction identities (cos²A and sin²A in terms of cos 2A). Most versatile formula in trig.

subject: Mathematics
title: Double Angle — tan
explanation: Derived from tan(A+A) using the addition formula. Undefined when tan²A = 1, i.e., A = 45°, 135°, etc. The denominator mirrors the cos 2A form in tan: cos 2A = (1−tan²A)/(1+tan²A).

subject: Mathematics
title: Power Reduction Identities
explanation: Derived from the double angle formulas for cos 2A. Used extensively in integration to reduce powers: ∫sin²x dx and ∫cos²x dx are solved using these identities.

subject: Mathematics
title: Triple Angle Formulas
latexCode: \begin{aligned} \sin 3A &= 3\sin A - 4\sin^3 A \\ \cos 3A &= 4\cos^3 A - 3\cos A \\ \tan 3A &= \frac{3\tan A - \tan^3 A}{1 - 3\tan^2 A} \end{aligned}
explanation: Triple angle formulas are derived by writing 3A = 2A + A and applying sum and double angle formulas. Used in cubic equation solving and Class 11–12 problems. Note sin3A starts with +3sinA but cos3A starts with +4cos³A.

subject: Mathematics
title: Half Angle — sin
explanation: Derived from cos A = 1 − 2sin²(A/2). The ± sign depends on the quadrant in which A/2 lies. For 0 < A < 180°, A/2 lies in Q1 so sin(A/2) is positive.

subject: Mathematics
title: Half Angle — cos
explanation: Derived from cos A = 2cos²(A/2) − 1. The ± sign depends on the quadrant of A/2. These formulas are used to find exact values like cos 22.5° = cos(45°/2) = √((1+cos45°)/2).

subject: Mathematics
title: Half Angle — tan (Three Forms)
latexCode: \begin{aligned} \tan\frac{A}{2} &= \pm\sqrt{\frac{1 - \cos A}{1 + \cos A}} \\ &= \frac{\sin A}{1 + \cos A} \\ &= \frac{1 - \cos A}{\sin A} \end{aligned}
explanation: The last two forms for tan(A/2) are sign-free (no ± needed) and are therefore more useful in equation solving and identity proving. Both are derived from sin and cos half-angle formulas.

subject: Mathematics
title: Weierstrass Substitution (t = tan A/2)
explanation: Expressing sin, cos, tan in terms of t = tan(A/2) is called Weierstrass (or t-) substitution. Used to convert trig integrals and equations into rational expressions in t. Critical for Class 12 integration.

subject: Mathematics
title: Product to Sum — sin·cos
explanation: Derived by adding/subtracting the sin addition formulas. Converts a product of sin and cos into a sum. Used in Fourier analysis and integrating products of trig functions.

subject: Mathematics
title: Product to Sum — cos·cos and sin·sin
explanation: Derived by adding/subtracting the cos addition formulas. 2cosAcosB gives a sum of cosines. 2sinAsinB gives a difference of cosines (note the order: A−B first). Used extensively in integration.

subject: Mathematics
title: Sum to Product — sin + sin and sin − sin
explanation: Obtained by substituting A = (C+D)/2, B = (C−D)/2 in the product-to-sum formulas. Sum of sines = 2sin(avg)cos(diff/2). Difference of sines = 2cos(avg)sin(diff/2).

subject: Mathematics
title: Sum to Product — cos + cos and cos − cos
explanation: Sum of cosines = 2cos(avg)cos(diff/2). Difference of cosines has a negative sign and involves sin: −2sin(avg)sin(diff/2). Note the minus sign in cos C − cos D — easily forgotten.

subject: Mathematics
title: Sum to Product — All Four Together
latexCode: \begin{aligned} \sin C + \sin D &= 2\sin\frac{C+D}{2}\cos\frac{C-D}{2} \\ \sin C - \sin D &= 2\cos\frac{C+D}{2}\sin\frac{C-D}{2} \\ \cos C + \cos D &= 2\cos\frac{C+D}{2}\cos\frac{C-D}{2} \\ \cos C - \cos D &= -2\sin\frac{C+D}{2}\sin\frac{C-D}{2} \end{aligned}
explanation: All four sum-to-product formulas in one place for quick reference. Memory tip: sin sum → sin·cos. sin diff → cos·sin. cos sum → cos·cos. cos diff → −sin·sin. The average angle always appears first.

subject: Mathematics
title: General Solutions of Trig Equations
explanation: General solutions for basic trig equations where n is any integer. These are essential for solving trig equations in Class 11. The sin formula uses alternating signs via (−1)ⁿ; cos and tan are simpler.

subject: Mathematics
title: Sine Rule
explanation: In triangle ABC, the ratio of each side to the sine of its opposite angle is constant and equals 2R (where R is the circumradius). Used to find unknown sides and angles in non-right triangles.

subject: Mathematics
title: Cosine Rule
latexCode: \begin{aligned} a^2 &= b^2 + c^2 - 2bc\cos A \\ \cos A &= \frac{b^2 + c^2 - a^2}{2bc} \end{aligned}
explanation: Generalises the Pythagorean theorem. When A = 90°, cos A = 0 and the formula reduces to a² = b² + c². Used when two sides and included angle (SAS) or all three sides (SSS) are known.

subject: Mathematics
title: Area of Triangle Using Trig
explanation: Area of a triangle = half × product of two sides × sine of the included angle. This formula works for any triangle (not just right-angled). It reduces to ½ base × height when the angle is 90°.

subject: Mathematics
title: Transforming a·sinθ + b·cosθ
explanation: Any expression of the form a·sinθ + b·cosθ can be written as a single sinusoidal function with amplitude R = √(a²+b²). Maximum value = R, minimum value = −R. Used in finding range and extrema.
searchTags: a sin theta b cos theta

subject: Mathematics
title: Definition of Logarithm
latexCode: \log_b a = x \iff b^x = a \quad (b > 0,\; b \neq 1,\; a > 0)
explanation: The logarithm is the inverse of exponentiation. log_b(a) = x means 'b raised to the power x gives a'. The base b must be positive and not equal to 1. The argument a must be strictly positive.

subject: Mathematics
title: Logarithm ↔ Exponential Conversion
explanation: To convert between log and exponential form: the base stays the base, the log value becomes the exponent, and the argument becomes the result. This conversion is the most fundamental skill in logarithms.

subject: Mathematics
title: Conditions for Logarithm to Exist
latexCode: \log_b a \text{ is defined only when:} \quad a > 0, \quad b > 0, \quad b \neq 1
explanation: You cannot take the log of zero or a negative number (in real numbers). The base cannot be 0, 1, or negative. These conditions must always be checked when solving log equations.

subject: Mathematics
title: Common Logarithm (Base 10)
explanation: When no base is written, it is assumed to be 10. Called the common logarithm. Used in pH calculations, Richter scale, and decibels. log 10 = 1, log 100 = 2, log 1000 = 3.

subject: Mathematics
title: Natural Logarithm (Base e)
explanation: The natural logarithm uses Euler's number e ≈ 2.718 as the base. Written as ln(a). Fundamental in calculus: d/dx(ln x) = 1/x and ∫(1/x)dx = ln|x| + C. ln e = 1, ln 1 = 0.

subject: Mathematics
title: Binary Logarithm (Base 2)
explanation: Base 2 logarithm used in computer science (bits, data structures, algorithms). log₂ 2 = 1, log₂ 4 = 2, log₂ 8 = 3, log₂ 1024 = 10. Time complexity O(log n) in algorithms typically means base 2.

subject: Mathematics
title: Log of 1 is Always Zero
explanation: Because b⁰ = 1 for any b. No matter what base is used, the log of 1 is always 0. This is one of the most-used results when simplifying log expressions.

subject: Mathematics
title: Log of Base Itself is Always One
explanation: Because b¹ = b. The log of any number to its own base is always 1. For example: log₂ 2 = 1, log₁₀ 10 = 1, ln e = 1. Used constantly for simplification.

subject: Mathematics
title: Exponential–Log Inverse Identity
explanation: Log and exponent with the same base cancel each other — they are inverse operations. First identity: b raised to log_b(a) = a. Second identity: log_b of b^x = x. Both are used to simplify expressions.

subject: Mathematics
title: Product Rule
explanation: The log of a product equals the sum of individual logs (with the same base). Extends to any number of factors: log(abc) = log a + log b + log c. This is the most-used log rule in simplification.

subject: Mathematics
title: Product Rule — Extended Form
latexCode: \log_b (a_1 \cdot a_2 \cdot a_3 \cdots a_n) = \log_b a_1 + \log_b a_2 + \log_b a_3 + \cdots + \log_b a_n
explanation: The product rule extends to any finite number of factors. All factors must be positive. Used to break down complex log expressions into simpler individual terms.

subject: Mathematics
title: Quotient Rule
explanation: The log of a quotient equals the difference of the individual logs. The order matters: log of numerator minus log of denominator. Used to simplify division inside a logarithm.

subject: Mathematics
title: Product & Quotient Rule Together
explanation: Combine both rules: numerator terms add, denominator terms subtract. Used to expand complex log expressions or to condense individual log terms back into a single logarithm.

subject: Mathematics
title: Power Rule
explanation: The exponent of the argument comes out as a multiplying factor in front of the log. This rule works for any real exponent n (positive, negative, or fractional). It is the key to solving equations where the variable is in an exponent.

subject: Mathematics
title: Power Rule — Negative Exponent
explanation: A direct consequence of the power rule with n = −1. The log of a reciprocal is the negative of the log. For example: log(1/2) = −log 2, ln(1/e) = −1.

subject: Mathematics
title: Root Rule — Square Root
explanation: The square root is a power of ½, so the power rule applies. The log of a square root is half the log of the number. For example: log√100 = ½ log 100 = ½ × 2 = 1.

subject: Mathematics
title: Root Rule — nth Root (General)
explanation: The nth root of m is m^(1/n). Applying the power rule gives (1/n)·log_b m. For cube root: log(∛m) = (1/3)log m. Works for any positive integer n.

subject: Mathematics
title: Root Rule — General Fractional Power
explanation: For any fractional exponent p/q, the power rule applies directly. This covers all root forms: ∛m² = m^(2/3) so log(∛m²) = (2/3)log m. Most general form of the root rule.

subject: Mathematics
title: Change of Base Formula
latexCode: \log_b a = \frac{\log_c a}{\log_c b} = \frac{\ln a}{\ln b} = \frac{\log a}{\log b}
explanation: Converts a logarithm from any base b to any other base c. Most commonly used to convert to base 10 (log) or base e (ln) for calculator computation. The new base c can be anything valid.

subject: Mathematics
title: Change of Base — Common Forms
latexCode: \begin{aligned} \log_b a &= \frac{\log a}{\log b} \quad \text{(to base 10)} \\ \log_b a &= \frac{\ln a}{\ln b} \quad \text{(to base e)} \\ \log_b a &= \frac{1}{\log_a b} \quad \text{(reciprocal form)} \end{aligned}
explanation: Three useful forms of the change of base formula. The reciprocal form log_b(a) = 1/log_a(b) is derived directly from change of base and is used in inequality problems and simplification.

subject: Mathematics
title: Reciprocal Relation
explanation: The log of a to base b and the log of b to base a are reciprocals of each other. For example: log₂ 8 = 3 and log₈ 2 = 1/3. Derived from the change of base formula with c = a.

subject: Mathematics
title: Chain Rule of Logarithms
explanation: When the argument of the first log equals the base of the second log, they collapse into a single log. Extends to any chain: log_a(b)·log_b(c)·log_c(d) = log_a(d). Useful for simplifying products of logs.

subject: Mathematics
title: Extended Chain Rule
explanation: The chain rule extends to any number of steps — each middle base cancels with the argument of the next term. The result is always log of the last argument to the first base. Very useful in olympiad and JEE problems.

subject: Mathematics
title: Log of a Power of the Base
explanation: Directly from the inverse identity. Any log where the argument is the base raised to a power immediately simplifies to that power. For example: log₂(2⁵) = 5, log(10³) = 3, ln(e⁷) = 7.

subject: Mathematics
title: Logarithm with Same Argument and Base
explanation: Two sides of the same inverse relationship. log_a(aˣ) = x because the log and the exponent cancel. a^(log_a x) = x for the same reason. Both are used to solve exponential and logarithmic equations directly.

subject: Mathematics
title: Logarithm of Zero and Negative Numbers
explanation: log of 0 is undefined because no power of b can equal 0. Log of a negative number is undefined in real numbers. Both conditions arise frequently in equations — always check the domain when solving.

subject: Mathematics
title: Inequality Behaviour of Logarithms
latexCode: \begin{aligned} b > 1: & \quad \log_b m > \log_b n \iff m > n \quad \text{(increasing)} \\ 0 < b < 1: & \quad \log_b m > \log_b n \iff m < n \quad \text{(decreasing)} \end{aligned}
explanation: For base > 1 (like log base 10 or ln), log is an increasing function — larger argument gives larger log. For 0 < base < 1, log is decreasing — the inequality FLIPS when taking log of both sides. Critical for solving log inequalities.

subject: Mathematics
title: Key Values to Memorise
explanation: These approximate values (all base 10 unless stated) are useful for estimation. Values for 4, 6, 8, 9 can be derived from log 2 and log 3 using the product and power rules — you only need to memorise log 2, log 3, and ln 2.

subject: Mathematics
title: Condensing Logs — Reverse Rules
latexCode: \begin{aligned} \log a + \log b &= \log(ab) \\ \log a - \log b &= \log\frac{a}{b} \\ n\log a &= \log(a^n) \end{aligned}
explanation: The product, quotient, and power rules work in reverse to condense multiple log terms into a single logarithm. Used in solving log equations: condense first, then convert to exponential form.
